title: Word2Vec Framework
learning_outcomes: Compare CBOW and Skip-Gram neural architectures.
Formulate projection layer representations and output softmax equations.
Explain why the projection layer is linear.

content_sections.title: Word2Vec Architectural Taxonomies
content_sections.content:
Word2Vec maps words to dense vector representations by training a shallow neural network on context window occurrences. It offers two architectures:

1.  **Skip-Gram:** Predicts surrounding context words given a target word $w_t$. Skip-gram works well with small training datasets and represents rare words effectively.
2.  **Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW):** Predicts a target word $w_t$ given its surrounding context words. CBOW is faster to train and shows slightly better performance on frequent words.

content_sections.title: Projection and Softmax Derivations
content_sections.content:
In both models, words are represented as one-hot vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$. The input projection matrix is $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$, and the output matrix is $\mathbf{W}' \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times V}$.

In the **Skip-Gram** model, given target word index $i$, the projected vector is:

$$\mathbf{v}_{w_i} = \mathbf{W}_{i, :}$$

To predict context word $w_o$, we compute the score $z = \mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_o} \mathbf{v}_{w_i}$ and pass it to a Softmax layer:

$$P(w_o \mid w_i) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_o} \mathbf{v}_{w_i})}{\sum_{w=1}^V \exp(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w} \mathbf{v}_{w_i})}$$

The projection layer contains no non-linear activation functions (it is linear). This is because the projection step acts as a simple lookup table, mapping sparse one-hot vectors to dense embeddings. Adding non-linear activations would slow down training without yielding better representations.
